differences.description: Document title changed from "The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual: Volume II" to "The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual, Volume II".
Release/version line changed from "Version 20250508: This document is in Ratified state." to "Version 20260120: Official Release".
The contributors sentence now ends with a single period after "Reinoud Zandijk"; the first document had a double period.
Footer document title changed from "The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual: Volume II" to "The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual, Volume II".
A new top-level preface for Version 20260120 was added, stating that the document contains the listed RISC-V ISA modules and that all have been ratified.
The module list now includes "Svrsw60t59b Extension" version 1.0.
A new change note was added: "Addition of the Svrsw60t59b extension for additional PTE reserved-for-software bits."
The 20250508 module table entry "Smctr" was renamed to "Smctr Extension".
The 20250508 module table entry "Svvptc" was renamed to "Svvptc Extension".
The 20250508 module table entry "Ssqosid" was renamed to "Ssqosid Extension".
The 20250508 module table entry "Pointer Masking" was renamed to "Pointer-Masking Extensions".
Change-list bullets for Ssqosid and Pointer Masking now end with periods and use "Pointer-Masking Extensions" rather than "Pointer Masking Extension".

The compatible-change bullet about PBMTE now says synchronization requirements were specified when changing both PBMTE and ADUE fields in menvcfg and henvcfg; the first document mentioned only PBMTE fields.
A compatible-change bullet was added: "Defined the format of the memory-mapped msip registers."
The mseccfg bullet changed from "Defined optional mseccfg and mseccfgh CSRs" to "Defined mseccfg and mseccfgh CSRs", removing the word "optional".
The description now refers to "The separate debug specification" instead of "The separate debug specification proposal".
The explanation of csr[9:8] now explicitly says that the pattern 10 represents hypervisor CSRs.

The CSR listing introduction now says Tables 4–7 list allocated CSR addresses instead of Tables 4–8, because the machine-level CSR listing was consolidated and indirect CSR mappings were added separately.

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: Supervisor indirect CSRs were added to the supervisor CSR list: siselect (0x150), sireg (0x151), sireg2 (0x152), sireg3 (0x153), sireg4 (0x155), sireg5 (0x156), and sireg6 (0x157).

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: Supervisor timer compare CSRs were added to the supervisor CSR list: stimecmp (0x14D) and stimecmph (0x15D, RV32 only).

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: The supervisor resource-management CSR srmcfg (0x181) was added to the supervisor CSR list.

differences.section: 2.2.2. Currently allocated RISC-V supervisor-level CSR addresses
differences.description: Supervisor Control Transfer Records CSRs were added to the supervisor CSR list: sctrctl (0x14E), sctrstatus (0x14F), and sctrdepth (0x15F).

differences.section: 2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses
differences.description: Virtual supervisor indirect CSRs were added to the hypervisor/VS CSR list: vsiselect (0x250), vsireg (0x251), vsireg2 (0x252), vsireg3 (0x253), vsireg4 (0x255), vsireg5 (0x256), and vsireg6 (0x257).

differences.section: 2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses
differences.description: Virtual supervisor timer compare CSRs were added to the hypervisor/VS CSR list: vstimecmp (0x24D) and vstimecmph (0x25D, RV32 only).

differences.section: 2.2.3. Currently allocated RISC-V hypervisor and VS CSR addresses
differences.description: The virtual supervisor Control Transfer Records CSR vsctrctl (0x24E) was added to the hypervisor/VS CSR list.

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: Machine indirect CSRs were added to the machine CSR list: miselect (0x350), mireg (0x351), mireg2 (0x352), mireg3 (0x353), mireg4 (0x355), mireg5 (0x356), and mireg6 (0x357).

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: Machine counter-configuration CSRs were added to the machine CSR list: mcyclecfg (0x321), minstretcfg (0x322), mcyclecfgh (0x721, RV32 only), and minstretcfgh (0x722, RV32 only).

differences.section: 2.2.4. Currently allocated RISC-V machine-level CSR addresses
differences.description: The Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register mctrctl (0x34E) was added to the machine CSR list.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind) mappings
differences.description: A new machine-mode indirect CSR mapping table was added for miselect values 0x30–0x3F, 0x70–0x7F, 0x80–0xBF, and 0xC0–0xFF, mapping AIA-related registers such as iprio*, eidelivery, eithreshold, eip*, and eie*.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind/Sscsrind) mappings
differences.description: A new S-mode indirect CSR mapping table was added, including AIA mappings, delegated counter mappings for siselect 0x40–0x5F, and CTR mappings for siselect 0x200–0x2FF.

differences.section: 2.2.5. Currently allocated RISC-V indirect CSR (Smcsrind/Sscsrind) mappings
differences.description: A new VS-mode indirect CSR mapping table was added, including AIA mappings and CTR mappings for vsiselect 0x200–0x2FF.
Commentary now says the architecture ID represents the microarchitecture associated with the "project" around which development occurs; the first document said "repo".
Requirement changed from "at least one hart must have a hart ID of zero" to "one hart must have a hart ID of zero".

differences.section: 3.1.6.2. Double Trap Control in mstatus Register
differences.description: The critical-error state description now specifies that whether performance counters and timers are updated in the critical-error state is UNSPECIFIED.

MPRV is now described as modifying the effective privilege mode for "explicit memory accesses" and normal behavior for "explicit memory accesses"; the first document described loads and stores specifically.
The TW description now consistently refers to execution in "modes less privileged than M" rather than "lower privilege modes" or "less-privileged modes".

The U-mode WFI rule now states that WFI in U-mode causes an illegal-instruction exception regardless of TW unless it completes within a bounded time; the first document did not include "regardless of the value of the TW bit" and included a future-revision note about selectively permitting WFI in U-mode, which was removed.

The MSIP description was changed to say memory-mapped control registers are used to provide machine-level interprocessor interrupts; the previous text described remote harts using the registers and a hart writing its own MSIP bit.

A new paragraph defines a hart’s memory-mapped msip register as a 32-bit read/write register with bits 31:1 reading as zero and bit 0 containing MSIP, and states updates are reflected in mip.MSIP eventually but not necessarily immediately.

The STIP/STIE description now incorporates Sstc behavior: if stimecmp is implemented, STIP is read-only in mip and reflects the stimecmp-generated supervisor timer interrupt signal; if stimecmp is not implemented, STIP remains writable for M-mode software delivery.
The RV32 counter access sentence now contains a typo "bitj 31-0" instead of "bits 31-0".

The TM bit is now specified to control access to stimecmp and vstimecmp from modes less privileged than M; clearing TM causes illegal-instruction exceptions, while setting TM permits access subject to hcounteren for VS-mode.
The list of exceptions for which nonzero mtval contains the faulting virtual address now includes hardware-error exceptions.
A new rule states that on a breakpoint exception raised by EBREAK or C.EBREAK, mtval is written with either zero or the virtual address of the instruction.
New commentary explains that recording the EBREAK instruction address in mtval is redundant with mepc but provided for backwards compatibility.
Descriptions of mtval for misaligned load/store faults and variable-length instruction fetch faults now include hardware-error exceptions in addition to access-fault and page-fault exceptions.
The mconfigptr alignment formula was made explicit: if MXLEN is 8×n, then mconfigptr[log2 n - 1:0] must be zero.

After changing menvcfg.ADUE, software must execute SFENCE.VMA with rs1=x0 and rs2=x0 to synchronize address-translation caches with the altered interpretation of PTE A/D bits; hypervisor additional synchronization is referenced.
The STCE field is now fully defined in the menvcfg section rather than merely stating that its definition is furnished by Sstc.

Detailed Zicboz CBZE semantics were added: CBZE enables CBO.ZERO in modes less privileged than M and otherwise CBO.ZERO raises an illegal-instruction exception; CBZE is read-only zero if Zicboz is not implemented.

Detailed Zicbom CBCFE semantics were added: CBCFE enables CBO.CLEAN and CBO.FLUSH in modes less privileged than M and otherwise they raise illegal-instruction exceptions; CBCFE is read-only zero if Zicbom is not implemented.
Detailed Zicbom CBIE semantics were added, including encodings 00b, 01b, 10b reserved, and 11b and their effects on CBO.INVAL execution in modes less privileged than M.
The PMM field is now fully defined in the menvcfg section with legal values for pointer masking under Smnpm; the first document only stated that the definition was furnished by Smnpm.
mseccfg is no longer described as an optional CSR; it is now a 64-bit read/write register that exists if any extension adding a field to mseccfg is implemented, otherwise it is reserved.
Detailed Zkr SSEED and USEED access-control semantics for the seed CSR were added, including U-mode, S/HS-mode, and VS/VU-mode behavior.
A new Entropy Source Access Control table was added for seed CSR access under M, U, S/HS, and VS/VU modes with SSEED and USEED settings.
Detailed Smepmp RLB semantics were added to the mseccfg section, including modification of locked PMP rules when RLB=1 and lockout behavior when RLB=0 and any pmpcfg.L bit is set.
Detailed Smepmp MMWP semantics were added to the mseccfg section, defining the sticky Machine-Mode Allowlist Policy bit and its effect of denying unmatched M-mode accesses.

Detailed Smepmp MML semantics were added to the mseccfg section, including changed pmpcfg.L meaning, M-mode-only and S/U-mode-only rules, Shared-Region encodings, restrictions on adding executable rules, and M-mode execution restrictions.
The PMM field is now fully defined in the mseccfg section with legal pointer-masking values under Smmpm; the first document only stated that the definition was furnished by Smmpm.

A note was added that Smmpm implementations need to satisfy max(largest supported virtual address size, largest supported supervisor physical address size) <= XLEN - PMLEN to avoid masking logic on the TLB access path.
The mseccfgh existence rule now explicitly says mseccfgh exists when XLEN=32 and mseccfg is implemented, and does not exist when XLEN=64.
Reset behavior now specifies that mseccfg.MML, mseccfg.MMWP, and mseccfg.RLB are reset to 0 unless the platform mandates a different reset value.
Reset behavior now specifies that mseccfg.USEED and mseccfg.SSEED must have defined reset values and must not be left undefined after reset.
AMOArithmetic now explicitly means all RISC-V AMOs defined by the A extension; the first document said all RISC-V AMOs.
Zacas AMO PMA support levels AMOCASW, AMOCASD, and AMOCASQ were added, defining support for AMOCAS.W, AMOCAS.D, and AMOCAS.Q.
A note was added that AMOCASW/D/Q require AMOArithmetic support because AMOCAS.W/D/Q require arithmetic comparison and swap capability.
A rule was added that AMOs specified by Zabha require the same level of support as the corresponding instructions in the A standard extension or Zacas extension.
The set of instructions to which the misaligned atomicity granule PMA applies now explicitly includes compressed encodings of the listed loads and stores.
PMP permission-fault text now explicitly covers cache-block management instructions as read accesses and cache-block zero instructions as write accesses.

A note was added explaining that a TOR PMP cannot represent address 2^(XLEN+2) as the top of a range, so in an RV32 system with 34-bit physical addresses the uppermost word cannot be exposed with TOR.
The sentence stating that if current XLEN is greater than MXLEN, PMP address registers are zero-extended from MXLEN to XLEN for address matching was removed.
The L bit permission text now says permissions are "additionally" enforced on M-mode accesses when L is set.
PMP matching and exception behavior was rewritten in terms of individual "memory operations" rather than "accesses", clarifying behavior for decomposed misaligned accesses and stores wider than XLEN.

RV32 state-enable CSR wording changed: the second document says there are CSR addresses for accessing upper 32 bits of corresponding machine-level and hypervisor CSRs, instead of saying all listed registers are 32-bit and have corresponding high-half CSRs.
The SRMCFG bit description now says it controls access to srmcfg introduced by the Ssqosid extension, without referencing a chapter number.

The introduction no longer names SMAP and SMEP; it now describes the two protective mechanisms generically and says RISC-V supports them through sstatus.SUM and supervisor execution denial for U-bit pages.
The former "6.1. Introduction" heading was removed; "Threat model" is now section 6.1 instead of 6.1.1.
A sentence was added stating that the rationale guiding Smepmp development is included in Appendix A.1.
Threat-model text now explicitly frames the absence of protections as "Without the Smepmp extension" and says attacks are mitigated through the virtual-memory system rather than by SMAP/SMEP.

The former "6.2. Proposal" section was replaced by a concise rules section that points to the mseccfg section for RLB, MMWP, MML field details; the detailed proposal text was moved to the mseccfg section.
The truth table for mseccfg.MML behavior was retained but is now introduced as a summary of physical memory protection rules rather than as subsection 6.2.1 of a proposal.
The visual representation of Smepmp rules is now included directly after the truth table without a separate subsection heading "6.2.2. Visual representation of the proposal".
The phrase "all fields defined on mseccfg as part of this proposal" changed to "all fields defined in mseccfg as part of this extension".
The Smepmp rationale was moved out of Chapter 6 into a new Appendix A.1 titled as historical rationale for the extension.
The first Smepmp rationale item now explicitly says a new mseccfg CSR was needed; the first document referred more generally to defining a new CSR.
The sentence listing the CSR numbers 0x321 for mcyclecfg, 0x322 for minstretcfg, 0x721 for mcyclecfgh, and 0x722 for minstretcfgh was removed from this section.
The mnepc masking description now says masking occurs for the implicit read by MNRET; the first document incorrectly said MRET.
Chapter title changed from "Smcdeleg" Counter Delegation Extension to "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions.
Introductory text now refers to "These extensions" rather than "This extension" and adds that for a RISC-V hardware platform Smcdeleg and Ssccfg must always be implemented in tandem.
The counter delegation text now says the Smcdeleg/Ssccfg "extensions" are enabled; the first document referred to a singular extension.

The note below the indirect HPM state mapping table was expanded into normative text: hpmeventi and cyclecfg/instretcfg may represent subsets, and MINH is read-only zero through sireg* when Sscofpmf or Smcntrpmf is implemented.
Separate later paragraphs stating the Sscofpmf and Smcntrpmf subset behavior for sireg2/sireg5 were removed because the content was moved immediately after the table.
The note identifying Smaia/Ssaia registers now includes mvip as defined by Smaia/Ssaia; the first document mentioned only mvien and hvien.
ctrtarget now specifies that for a not-taken branch it holds the PC of the next sequential instruction following the branch.
The commentary now refers to "Transfer Type Filter bits" instead of "Transfer Type Filtering bits".

The STIP/STIE description now incorporates Sstc behavior: when Sstc is implemented, STIP reflects the interrupt signal from stimecmp and is set or cleared based on writes to stimecmp relative to time.

A new supervisor CSR section was added for stimecmp/stimecmph, defining the 64-bit supervisor timer compare register, RV32 high-half access, STIP posting condition, eventual reflection, and spurious timer interrupt behavior.
Detailed Zicboz CBZE semantics were added for U-mode CBO.ZERO execution, including dependency on S-mode enablement and read-only-zero behavior if Zicboz is not implemented.
Detailed Zicbom CBCFE semantics were added for U-mode CBO.CLEAN and CBO.FLUSH execution, including dependency on S-mode enablement and read-only-zero behavior if Zicbom is not implemented.
Detailed Zicbom CBIE semantics were added for U-mode CBO.INVAL execution, including reserved encoding 10b, S-mode enablement dependency, and behavior for encodings 01b and 11b.
The PMM field is now fully defined in the senvcfg section with legal pointer-masking values under Ssnpm; the first document only stated that the definition was furnished by Ssnpm.

The commentary on stale translations after PTE modification was expanded: it now says any translation since the last subsuming SFENCE.VMA may be used, subsequent accesses may use different translations, and translations with different virtual-address widths may be used.

A new rule states that no SFENCE.VMA is required after enabling or disabling pointer masking because pointer masking applies only to the effective address and does not affect memory-management data structures.
Page-permission fault rules now explicitly include cache-block management instructions as load-like accesses and cache-block zero instructions as store-like accesses.
The A/D update rule now says the atomic PTE update must perform all "page-table walk checks" rather than all "tablewalk checks".
Step 9 now specifies that the PMA/PMP check is for a store to the PTE at address a+va.vpn[i]×PTESIZE, rather than generically a store to pte.
Step 9 now explicitly says that after setting pte.a and possibly pte.d, the implementation stores pte to the PTE address; the first document omitted the explicit store step.
Sv48 leaf page sizes are now explicitly listed as 4 KiB pages, 2 MiB megapages, 1 GiB gigapages, and 512 GiB terapages; the first document used only generic page-size names.
Sv57 leaf page sizes are now explicitly listed as 4 KiB pages, 2 MiB megapages, 1 GiB gigapages, 512 GiB terapages, and 256 TiB petapages; the first document used only generic page-size names.
Svnapot was moved from standalone Chapter 13 to Section 12.7 under the Supervisor-Level ISA chapter.
A new sentence states that if the hypervisor extension is implemented, Svnapot is also supported in G-stage translation.
Svpbmt was moved from standalone Chapter 14 to Section 12.8 under the Supervisor-Level ISA chapter.
Svinval was moved from standalone Chapter 15 to Section 12.9 under the Supervisor-Level ISA chapter.
The exception-permission sentence was split so the U-mode illegal-instruction rule is a separate sentence before the mstatus.TVM rule; semantics are otherwise unchanged.
Svadu was moved from standalone Chapter 16 to Section 12.10 under the Supervisor-Level ISA chapter.
Svvptc was moved from standalone Chapter 17 to Section 12.11 under the Supervisor-Level ISA chapter.

differences.section: 12.12. Svrsw60t59b Extension for PTE Reserved-for-Software Bits 60-59
differences.description: A new Svrsw60t59b extension section was added, reserving PTE bits 60–59 for supervisor software and requiring implementations to ignore them.

differences.section: 12.12. Svrsw60t59b Extension for PTE Reserved-for-Software Bits 60-59
differences.description: The new Svrsw60t59b section specifies that if the Hypervisor extension is implemented, bits 60–59 of G-stage PTEs are reserved for supervisor software and ignored by the implementation.

differences.section: 12.12. Svrsw60t59b Extension for PTE Reserved-for-Software Bits 60-59
differences.description: The new Svrsw60t59b section states that the extension depends on Sv39 and explains that operating systems use reserved PTE bits for metadata, avoiding auxiliary lookups.
Ssqosid was moved from standalone Chapter 18 to Section 12.13 under the Supervisor-Level ISA chapter.

The Sstc chapter was condensed: detailed machine/supervisor/hypervisor additions were removed from the chapter and replaced with references to the stimecmp, vstimecmp, menvcfg, and henvcfg sections where the normative CSR behavior now appears.
The separate Sstc subsection "Machine and Supervisor Level Additions" and its subsections for stimecmp, mip/mie, sip/sie, and mcounteren were removed from the Sstc chapter.
The separate Sstc subsection "Hypervisor Extension Additions" and its subsections for vstimecmp, hip/hie, and hcounteren were removed from the Sstc chapter.
The separate Sstc subsection "Environment Config (menvcfg and henvcfg) Support" was removed from the Sstc chapter; STCE behavior is now specified in menvcfg and henvcfg sections.
Sscofpmf was renumbered from Chapter 20 to Chapter 14 because supervisor virtual-memory extensions and Ssqosid were folded into Chapter 12.
The Hypervisor extension was renumbered from Chapter 21 to Chapter 15.

The hstatus.HUPMM field is now defined for Ssnpm, controlling pointer masking for HLV.* and HSV.* instructions in U-mode when their explicit memory access is performed as VU-mode; read-only-zero behavior for RV32 and when Ssnpm is absent is specified.
A note was added recommending that the hypervisor copy the guest senvcfg.PMM value to hstatus.HUPMM before invoking HLV.* or HSV.* instructions in U-mode.

differences.section: 15.2.2. Hypervisor Trap Delegation (hedeleg and hideleg) Registers
differences.description: Figure caption corrected from "Hypervisor exception delegation register (hideleg)" to "Hypervisor interrupt delegation register (hideleg)".

differences.section: 15.2.3. Hypervisor Interrupt (hvip, hip, and hie) Registers
differences.description: The VSTIP description now incorporates Sstc behavior: VSTIP is the OR of hvip.VSTIP and, when Sstc is implemented, the timer interrupt signal from vstimecmp; it specifies set/clear behavior based on vstimecmp compared with time+htimedelta.
The STCE field is now fully defined in the henvcfg section rather than merely stating that its definition is furnished by Sstc.
Detailed Zicboz CBZE semantics were added for CBO.ZERO in VS/VU modes, including HS-qualified checks and virtual-instruction exception behavior.
Detailed Zicbom CBCFE semantics were added for CBO.CLEAN and CBO.FLUSH in VS/VU modes, including HS-qualified checks and virtual-instruction exception behavior.
Detailed Zicbom CBIE semantics were added for CBO.INVAL in VS/VU modes, including HS-qualified checks, reserved encoding 10b, encodings 01b and 11b, and flush/invalidate behavior.
The PMM field is now fully defined in the henvcfg section with legal pointer-masking values for VS-mode under Ssnpm; the first document only stated that the definition was furnished by Ssnpm.
The TM bit is now specified to control VS-mode access to vstimecmp via stimecmp, causing virtual-instruction exceptions when hcounteren.TM is clear and mcounteren.TM is set.

differences.section: 15.2.18. Virtual Supervisor Address Translation and Protection (vsatp) Register
differences.description: The note about HLV/HLVX/HSV and VS-stage A bits now says mispredicted virtual-machine load/store instructions must not set VS-stage A bits; the first document said misspeculatively executed instructions.

A new hypervisor CSR section was added for vstimecmp/vstimecmph, defining the 64-bit virtual supervisor timer compare register, RV32 high-half access, VSTIP posting condition, and eventual reflection.

differences.section: 15.3.1. Hypervisor Virtual-Machine Load and Store Instructions
differences.description: The section now states that HLVX.* memory accesses are not subject to pointer masking.

differences.section: 15.3.1. Hypervisor Virtual-Machine Load and Store Instructions
differences.description: A note was added explaining that HLVX.* is designed to emulate implicit instruction fetch accesses from guest memory, so pointer masking does not apply; the same reason is given for MXR disabling pointer masking.
The G bit in G-stage PTEs is now described as "currently not used" rather than "reserved for future standard use"; software must still clear it for forward compatibility and hardware must ignore it.
Synchronization text now covers changes to both PBMTE and ADUE bits in menvcfg and henvcfg; the first document covered PBMTE only.
The world-switch note now refers to henvcfg.PBMTE/ADUE needing to be world-switched; the first document mentioned only henvcfg.PBMTE.
A new hypervisor section was added for pointer masking interaction with two-stage translation, including cases requiring HFENCE.GVMA when henvcfg.PMM changes and XLEN-PMLEN is less than GPA width.
In the hypervisor trap cause table, interrupt exception code 13 changed from "Reserved for counter-overflow interrupt" to "Counter-overflow interrupt".
The Control-flow Integrity chapter was renumbered from Chapter 22 to Chapter 16.

differences.section: 16.1.2. Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps
differences.description: The sentence about LPAD now says "a software-check exception caused by the LPAD instruction itself"; the first document said "an software-check exception" and referenced "[LP_INST]".

Bare-mode shadow-stack behavior was narrowed: the second document says shadow stack instructions raise store/AMO access-fault exceptions when satp.MODE or vsatp.MODE is Bare and effective privilege is below M; the first document said shadow stack memory accesses are prohibited and shadow stack instructions raise such exceptions.
The commentary now says "access fault" instead of "access-fault" in the phrase about triggering an access fault instead of a page fault.
Ssdbltrp was renumbered from Chapter 23 to Chapter 17.
Pointer Masking was renumbered from Chapter 24 to Chapter 18.
The sentence about tools applying in privilege modes now uses "U, S, and M" with an Oxford comma; the first document used "U, S and M".

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: The compressed-instruction list no longer includes C.LQSP and C.SQSP; the first document listed them among compressed instructions subject to pointer masking.

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: Hardware writes to CSRs and debug trigger matching now apply pointer masking only "when applicable"; the first document stated pointer masking is applied without that qualifier.

differences.section: 18.2.6. Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking
differences.description: The stval example now says hardware applies pointer masking to an address written into stval if pointer masking is applicable to that address; the first document said pointer masking will be applied to any address written into stval.
The descriptions of Ssnpm, Smnpm, and Smmpm now include cross-references to the CSR sections that define their controls; the first document directed readers to Section 24.3.

The standalone Section 24.3 "ISA Extensions" and its subsections Ssnpm, Smnpm, Smmpm, Interaction with SFENCE.VMA, Interaction with Two-Stage Address Translation, and Number of Masked Bits were removed as a separate section; their normative content was moved into CSR, SFENCE.VMA, and hypervisor sections.
The standalone PMM WARL field value table was removed from the Pointer Masking chapter; equivalent PMM tables now appear in menvcfg, mseccfg, senvcfg, and henvcfg sections.
The "Number of Masked Bits" material was moved from Section 24.3.6 under ISA Extensions to Section 18.2.8 under Background.
The instruction listing now includes a new "Control Transfer Records Management Instructions" category with SCTRCLR encoding 0001000 00100 00000 000 00000 1110011.
The History chapter was renumbered from Chapter 26 to Chapter 20.
The Bibliography is no longer a standalone final section; in the second document it appears at the end of Appendix A text rather than as a separate parsed page/section.

differences.section: Appendix A: Historical Rationale for Extensions
differences.description: A new appendix was added explaining that extension rationales are ordered chronologically, will be added over time for older extensions, and may be synthesized from historical records if not recorded.
